title: The Meritorious Price of Our Redemption
description: A Puritan theological work that triggered one of colonial New England's earliest book suppressions.
descriptionParagraphs: A Puritan theological work that triggered one of colonial New England's earliest book suppressions.

The Meritorious Price of Our Redemption is organized less as a story than as an argument. As a theological tract, it tries to persuade readers through selection, emphasis, and direct claims about theology, colonial law, and heresy.

Its significance lies in the way it compresses large claims into memorable formulas and positions. Even readers who reject the work usually have to reckon with how sharply it frames questions about theology, colonial law, and heresy.

cover.alt: The Meritorious Price of Our Redemption cover thumbnail from Wikipedia

overview.englishDescription: A Puritan theological work that triggered one of colonial New England's earliest book suppressions.
banningParagraphs: The Meritorious Price of Our Redemption entered censorship debates as a theological tract associated with theology, colonial law, and heresy. In the current dossier, the main state objections cluster around heresy and doctrinal control.

The earliest event currently captured here is 1650 in United States, where Massachusetts Bay colonial authorities ordered burned. Colonial magistrates condemned the tract as theologically unsound. It is one of the earliest English-language American examples of the state destroying a book.
This entry is still incomplete: more jurisdictions, court orders, and translated justifications should be added over time.

counterReadings.title: Assassins of the Mind
counterReadings.author: Christopher Hitchens
counterReadings.note: Frames the Rushdie affair as a test of free speech against violent religious intimidation.

counterReadings.title: From Fatwa to Jihad
counterReadings.author: Kenan Malik
counterReadings.note: Tracks how conflicts over blasphemy, race, and offense evolved after the Rushdie controversy.

counterReadings.title: 100 Banned Books: Censorship Histories of World Literature
counterReadings.author: Nicholas J. Karolides, Margaret Bald, and Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: A compact reference on how censorship systems moved across states, churches, and courts.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: 387 B.C. to 1978 A.D.
counterReadings.author: Anne Lyon Haight
counterReadings.note: Useful for comparing older obscenity, heresy, and political bans with modern free-speech disputes.
